results.message: What's the MIG duty cycle at 200 amps on 240 volt input?
results.answerPreview: _Searching manuals..._ _Found the duty-cycle table and setup guide._ At **200 amps on 240V input**, the MIG duty cycle is **25%**. That means you can weld for about **2.5 minutes**, then must rest **7.5 minutes** in every 10-minute period to avoid overheating the machine. The int

arguments.answer:
At **200 amps on 240V input**, the MIG duty cycle is **25%**.

That means you can weld for about **2.5 minutes**, then must rest **7.5 minutes** in every 10-minute period to avoid overheating the machine.

The interactive calculator shows the rated duty point and rest periods for your requested amperage.
safetyNotices: Exceeding the duty cycle can overheat and damage the welder. Always allow the full rest period before resuming.
If the thermal overload trips, let the machine cool completely before restarting.
suggestedActions: Use the duty-cycle calculator in the workspace to explore other amperage levels
If you need longer continuous welds, consider reducing amperage (100% duty at 115A on 240V MIG)

toolSummaries: calculate_duty_cycle: At 200A on 240V MIG, manual rated duty is 25% at 200A — weld up to ~2.5 min per 10 min, then rest ~7.5 min.

results.answerPreview: _Searching manuals..._ _Found relevant manual sections._ Based on the manual evidence retrieved (p.24, p.13, p.8, p.27), here is the best supported guidance for: "For TIG on this machine, which polarity and which socket should the work clamp go into?". See the workspace artifact

arguments.spec:
{
  "type": "manual-figure",
  "caption": "TIG Setup: Ground Clamp in Positive Socket, TIG Torch in Negative Socket",
  "source": "owner-manual.pdf",
  "page": 24,
  "assetId": "manual-assets/owner-manual/p24.png"
}

arguments.answer: For TIG welding on the OmniPro 220, connect the **work clamp (ground clamp) to the POSITIVE (+) socket**. The TIG torch goes into the NEGATIVE (−) socket. This is DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative) polarity, which is standard for TIG welding most materials. Twist each connector clockwise all the way to lock in place.

citations.excerpt: 1. Plug Ground Clamp Cable into Positive Socket. Twist clockwise all the way to lock in place. 2. Plug TIG Torch Cable (TIG Torch sold separately) into Negative Socket. Twist clockwise all the way to lock in place.

safetyNotices: Turn the Power Switch off and unplug the Welder before setup
suggestedActions: Ensure Power Switch is OFF before connecting cables
Twist connectors clockwise all the way to lock
Connect shielding gas after cable setup

results.message: I'm welding 1/8 inch mild steel with MIG solid wire on 240V — what settings and wire should I use?
results.answerPreview: _Searching manuals..._ _Found the settings chart and setup guide._ For 1/8" mild steel with MIG solid wire at 240V: **Polarity:** DCEP (electrode positive) **Wire:** Gas-shielded solid core (e.g., ER70S-6) **Shielding Gas:** 75% Ar / 25% CO₂ or 100% CO₂ **Settings:** The exact vo

arguments.answer:
For 1/8" mild steel with MIG solid wire at 240V:

**Polarity:** DCEP (electrode positive)  
**Wire:** Gas-shielded solid core (e.g., ER70S-6)  
**Shielding Gas:** 75% Ar / 25% CO₂ or 100% CO₂

**Settings:** The exact voltage and wire-speed values are on the **Settings Chart inside your welder door** (row for 1/8" mild steel). Values depend on your wire diameter — common sizes are .030", .035", or .045".

The interactive settings chart is now displayed. Once you confirm your wire diameter, I can point you to the specific row on the door chart.
arguments.clarifyingQuestion: What wire diameter are you using? (Common sizes: .030", .035", or .045")

artifact.title: MIG Settings: 1/8" Mild Steel @ 240V
artifact.note: Numeric voltage and wire-speed values vary by wire diameter — refer to the chart row matching your wire size.

citations.excerpt: Recommended wire speed and voltage values — door chart (image; requires chart lookup or multimodal interpretation).
Refer to the Settings Chart on the inside of the Welder door.
Weldable Materials: Mild Steel, Stainless Steel, Aluminum (with optional Spool Gun).

safetyNotices: DCEP polarity required for solid-core MIG — incorrect polarity causes porosity and poor penetration.
Shielding gas must flow (10–25 CFH) — insufficient gas causes porosity.
Clean workpiece of rust, paint, oil before welding.
suggestedActions: Check the Settings Chart inside the welder door for your wire diameter row at 1/8" mild steel.
Confirm wire spool is ER70S-6 or equivalent solid mild-steel wire.
Verify gun is connected to positive (+) terminal (DCEP).
Set gas regulator to 15–20 CFH and confirm flow at the nozzle.

results.message: Give me exact MIG voltage and WFS for 3/8 inch titanium on 480V three-phase.
results.answerPreview: _Searching manuals..._ _Found the settings chart and setup guide._ **[Conflicting manual sources]** Conflicting manual sources: Answer mixes 120 V and 240 V specifications without clarifying which input is in use. **The OmniPro 220 cannot operate on 480V three-phase power, and it

arguments.query: input voltage 480V three-phase power supply specifications

arguments.answer:
**The OmniPro 220 cannot operate on 480V three-phase power, and it has no settings for titanium.**

This machine accepts only **120 VAC or 240 VAC single-phase** input (owner-manual.pdf p.7, specifications table). It does not support 480V three-phase.

The manual's weldable materials are **mild steel, stainless steel, and aluminum** (owner-manual.pdf p.7). There are no door-chart settings for titanium—titanium typically requires TIG with specialized inert atmosphere and is outside this machine's design envelope.

**If you're working with 3/8″ mild steel or stainless on 240V**, I can provide exact voltage and wire-feed-speed settings from the selection chart. Let me know the correct material and I'll look it up.
arguments.clarifyingQuestion: Are you welding mild steel, stainless, or aluminum (the three materials this machine supports), and will you be using 120V or 240V power?

results.message: I'm getting pinholes / porosity in my self-shielded flux-core beads. What should I check first?
results.answerPreview: _Searching manuals..._ _Found relevant manual sections._ For self-shielded flux-core porosity, check these in order: **1. Polarity (most common cause)** — Self-shielded flux-core requires DCEN (electrode negative). If your gun lead is on the positive (+) terminal, you'll get seve

arguments.evidenceSummary: User confirmed self-shielded flux-core (no external gas used), so shielding gas flow causes do not apply.
arguments.questionRationale: Polarity is the #1 cause of porosity in self-shielded flux-core and easiest to verify. Self-shielded typically requires DCEN (electrode negative).

arguments.answer:
For self-shielded flux-core porosity, check these in order:

**1. Polarity (most common cause)** — Self-shielded flux-core requires DCEN (electrode negative). If your gun lead is on the positive (+) terminal, you'll get severe porosity. Verify gun → negative (–), work clamp → positive (+).

**2. Workpiece contamination** — Clean off all rust, mill scale, paint, oil, grease, or moisture. Even light surface rust can cause pinholes.

**3. Stickout too long** — Keep contact-tip-to-work distance (CTWD) around 3/4″. Excessive stickout weakens the flux shielding.

**4. Wind/drafts** — Even self-shielded wire can be disrupted by strong cross-drafts. Shield the weld area if outdoors or near fans.

What polarity are you currently using?
arguments.clarifyingQuestion: What polarity are you currently using — is your gun lead connected to the negative (–) terminal or positive (+) terminal?

citations.excerpt: Porosity in the Weld Metal porosity pinholes gas pockets porous cavities holes in bead the weld metal Shielding gas bottle is empty. Not enough or too much shielding gas. Dirty workpiece. Gun is being used too far away from workpiece. Polarity is incorrect for the application

Porosity Wire Weld – Porosity pinholes gas pockets porous cavities holes in bead Incorrect polarity insufficient shielding gas (MIG only) dirty workpiece or welding wire inconsistent travel speed CTWD too long.

suggestedActions: Verify polarity: gun lead to negative (–) terminal, work clamp to positive (+)
Wire-brush workpiece to bare metal, removing all rust and contaminants
Check stickout length — should be approximately 3/4 inch
Shield weld area from wind or cross-drafts if working outdoors

topHypotheses.label: Polarity is incorrect (should be DCEN for self-shielded)
topHypotheses.nextCheck: Verify gun lead is on negative (–) terminal

topHypotheses.label: Dirty workpiece
topHypotheses.nextCheck: Clean to bare metal with wire brush

topHypotheses.label: Gun too far from workpiece (CTWD too long)

ruledOut: Shielding gas issues (not applicable to self-shielded wire)

telemetry.clarifyingQuestion: What polarity are you currently using — is your gun lead connected to the negative (–) terminal or positive (+) terminal?

results.message: It's gasless flux core on 1/8 mild steel, DCEN, and the wire looks clean. Stickout is about 3/4 inch.
results.answerPreview: _Searching manuals..._ _Found relevant manual sections._ Your DCEN polarity is correct for gasless flux-cored wire (Ground Clamp to + socket, Wire Feed Power to – socket per page 13). Wire cleanliness is good. However, your **3/4" stickout is excessive** for flux-cored welding. P

arguments.answer:
Your DCEN polarity is correct for gasless flux-cored wire (Ground Clamp to + socket, Wire Feed Power to – socket per page 13). Wire cleanliness is good.

However, your **3/4" stickout is excessive** for flux-cored welding. Proper stickout for gasless flux-core should be **1/2" to 5/8"** maximum. Excessive stickout causes wire preheating, inconsistent arc, poor penetration, and increased spatter.

**What specific welding problem are you experiencing?** (Examples: excessive spatter, poor penetration, porosity, unstable arc, wire burn-back, etc.) This will help narrow down whether stickout is the primary cause or if settings/technique need adjustment.
arguments.clarifyingQuestion: What specific weld defect or arc behavior are you trying to fix? (e.g., spatter, porosity, poor penetration, unstable arc, wire feeding issues)

citations.excerpt: 16. DCEN Direct Current Electrode Negative Wire Setup for Flux-Cored (gasless) welding: Plug Ground Clamp Cable into Positive ( + ) Socket. Plug Wire Feed Power Cable into Negative ( – ) Socket. Twist cables clockwise all the way to lock in place.

suggestedActions: Reduce stickout to 1/2" - 5/8" and re-test
Describe the specific weld defect or arc problem you're seeing
